mechanism: Domain-specific further pre-training on the Beige Book corpus and supervised fine-tuning on 800 human-labelled chunks produces a representation and decision boundary tuned to the target task, raising accuracy above zero-shot LLM performance.

Fine-tuning on labelled three-class data calibrates the predicted class distribution to the human label distribution, eliminating the hedging bias of zero-shot prompting that pushes ambiguous passages toward the mixed class.

Architecture family alone (lexical vs. pre-trained LLM vs. fine-tuned BERT) is associated with F1 ranges, but within the pre-trained LLM family there is large variance unrelated to architecture (e.g., Llama 8B > GPT-4o on this task), implying training data and prompt sensitivity matter more than architecture per se.

Beige Book narratives aggregate qualitative reports from business contacts across 12 Federal Reserve districts; positive sentiment co-occurs with stronger contemporaneous real-economy activity. The signal carries forward 2-3 Beige-Book periods because anecdotal turning points often lead published macro statistics.
Negative Beige Book sentiment is generated primarily by reports of weak hiring, layoffs, and slack labour markets, which align mechanically with rising unemployment.
Strong-demand environments produce both optimistic Beige Book commentary and upward price pressure; the positive correlation reflects this co-movement rather than sentiment causing inflation.

Fine-tuning shifts compute cost from inference to a one-time training phase. BeigeSage required ~39 minutes of further pre-training and ~5 hours 14 minutes of fine-tuning, but then classified 200 chunks in 1 minute 35 seconds, comparable to GPT-4o (remote) and faster than locally-run Llama (~9 min) and Gemma (~16 min).
